chunk_summary: Guidance on dairy elimination in vegetarian families, focusing on protein, calcium, and vitamin D replacement, with attention to nutrient gaps and supplementation in children.
learning_objectives: Understand the nutritional challenges of dairy elimination in vegetarian diets.
Learn strategies for protein and calcium replacement in children over 1 year.
Identify secondary nutrient gaps such as vitamin B12 and iodine in strict vegetarian diets.
Develop monitoring plans for growth and dietary adequacy during dairy elimination.
key_facts: Dairy elimination creates gaps in protein, energy, calcium, and vitamin D intake.
Plant-based beverages are not nutritionally equivalent to milk unless fortified with protein, calcium, and vitamin D.
Calcium-rich foods like ragi and sesame can help but may not meet dietary equivalence in food-insecure settings.
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation is recommended when dietary targets are unlikely to be met.
Growth and dietary reassessment should occur within 2–4 weeks of starting dairy elimination.
Strict vegetarian diets may require monitoring for vitamin B12 and iodine deficiencies.
reference_quote: Plant-based beverages are not nutritionally equivalent to milk unless they are specifically fortified and provide meaningful protein; many commercially available options are essentially flavored water. (Source: WAO DRACMA guideline update)
document_key_topics: Supplementation for vitamin D and B12
